citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
A conversation with the attending veterinarian revealed that the licensee has not given the proper authority to ensure
animal health and well-being to the attending veterinarian. The attending veterinarian stated that all of the “SOP’s and
contingency plans that have been prepared by her for the facility, the facility will not accept or are too busy to discuss”.
The attending veterinarian expressed that she has given verbal directions to the licensee but they have relayed to her that
“they know better” and continue their own practices and treatments on matters of animal health.
During the conversation it was not known to the attending veterinarian that the licensee is currently treating a tiger with a
medication that she did not approve prior to administration.
Facilities regulated under the Animal Welfare Act must hire an attending veterinarian and give them adequate authority to
ensure animal health and well-being. Which also includes frequent and concise communication between the facility and
the attending veterinarian.
The licensee shall assure that the attending veterinarian has appropriate authority to ensure the provision of adequate
veterinary care and to oversee the adequacy of other aspects of animal care and use.
To be corrected by:3.6.2026

citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
At time of inspection a Siberian female Tiger (Amal mc 990000006449250) was exhibiting hair loss and scabbing at the 3-
4 inches of the tail. The facility representative stated that the tiger has been sucking and overgrooming the tip of her tail.
The facility noticed the condition and started the tiger on Fluoxetine to address the behavior and started a topical spray to
use on the tail on November 28, 2025.
However, the Attending Veterinarian (AV) was not consulted for approval prior to starting the Fluoxetine or the use of the
hypochlorous and vinegar spray. The AV stated that the facility representative first contacted her about the tiger’s tail on
2/26/26 and she did not approve the use of the hypochlorous and vinegar spray and advised the facility representative to
switch to MiconaHex-Triz spray. To date, the attending veterinarian stated that she has not seen the tiger’s tail.
The tiger’s tail condition could be due to a variety of medical conditions. Not consulting with the attending veterinarian
prior to administering treatment for the tiger increases the risk of using inappropriate methods to diagnose, control, and
treat the animal’s condition.
The licensee must have this animal examined by a licensed veterinarian in order to ensure that an accurate diagnosis is
obtained and appropriate treatment plans are developed and followed.
The licensee must ensure that all animals receive adequate veterinary care at all times.
To Be Corrected By: March 11, 2026
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with Licensee.
n

2.40(a)(2) 
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
A conversation with the attending veterinarian revealed that the licensee has not given the proper authority to ensure

The attending veterinarian expressed that she has given verbal directions to the licensee but they have relayed to her that

 During the conversation it was not known to the attending veterinarian that the licensee is currently treating a tiger with a 
medication that she did not approve prior to administration.  
Facilities regulated under the Animal Welfare Act must hire an attending veterinarian and give them adequate authority to 
ensure animal health and well-being. Which also includes frequent and concise communication between the facility and

2.40(b)(2) 
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
At time of inspection a Siberian female Tiger (Amal mc 990000006449250) was exhibiting hair loss and scabbing at the 3-
4 inches of the tail. The facility representative stated that the tiger has been sucking and overgrooming the tip of her tail. 
The facility noticed the condition and started the tiger on Fluoxetine to address the behavior and started a topical spray to 
use on the tail on November 28, 2025. 
However, the Attending Veterinarian (AV) was not consulted for approval prior to starting the Fluoxetine or the use of the 
hypochlorous and vinegar spray. The AV stated that the facility representative first contacted her about the tiger’s tail on 
2/26/26 and she did not approve the use of the hypochlorous and vinegar spray and advised the facility representative to 
switch to MiconaHex-Triz spray. To date, the attending veterinarian stated that she has not seen the tiger’s tail. 
The tiger’s tail condition could be due to a variety of medical conditions. Not consulting with the attending veterinarian 
prior to administering treatment for the tiger increases the risk of using inappropriate methods to diagnose, control, and 
treat the animal’s condition.  
The licensee must have this animal examined by a licensed veterinarian in order to ensure that an accurate diagnosis is 
obtained and appropriate treatment plans are developed and followed.

The licensee must ensure that all animals receive adequate veterinary care at all times. 
To Be Corrected By: March 11, 2026 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with Licensee.
